interactions.summary: Jiang asks whether Jay has been to Israel, Jay distinguishes his teenage Jewish trip from Birthright, and Jiang explains that a 2012 Technion visit gave him a moving, propaganda-powerful Israel tour including Masada and the Holocaust Museum.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Jiang asks and answers; Jay responds.

interactions.summary: Jiang explains the dissonance behind his Holocaust comments: after reading Arendt and absorbing the Holocaust Museum narrative, he expected bureaucratic extermination documentation, searched for speeches and direct evidence online, could not find what satisfied him, and therefore made the controversial lecture statement; Jay answers that this will still leave people unsatisfied because mythology outruns truth.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_00 is Jiang; SPEAKER_01 is Jay. seg-0048 begins Jay's response and continues into the next packet's deeper Holocaust-language discussion.

claims.claim: Jiang says his 2012 Technion-linked Israel visit was emotionally powerful and that Israeli tour guides and institutions have a strong capacity for propaganda and narrative.
claims.temporal_scope: Biographical source of later Israel interpretation, stated 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jiang says his confusion over Holocaust evidence came from expecting documents matching a systematic bureaucratic extermination mission and not finding direct evidence online that satisfied that expectation.
claims.temporal_scope: Explanation of prior lecture controversy given 2026-04-01.

claims.claim: Jay maintains that even Jiang's explanation will not satisfy critics because mythologized history can cause people to question whether the underlying truth is true at all.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang says the Israeli tour guide brought them to tears, then later calls that same power propaganda; the emotional success of the story becomes evidence of its political force.
signature_moments.source_phrase: one of the most moving experiences of my life
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It shows why he takes myth seriously: not because it is merely false, but because it can move bodies and loyalties.

signature_moments.moment: Jiang's controversy begins as a mismatch between an expected bureaucratic paper trail and a web search that fails to satisfy him.
signature_moments.why_it_matters: It clarifies the epistemic error Jay is probing: internet absence becomes too much weight inside a charged historical question.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Jiang's term for Israel's capacity to convert sites like Masada and the Holocaust Museum into emotionally powerful historical story.
Jiang's contested standard for Holocaust evidence in the lecture controversy; the packet treats it as his stated criterion, not as a validated historical judgment.

chronology_notes.note: Jiang dates his Israel trip to 2012, when Technion invited him as a well-known educator in China.

uncertainty_notes.note: This packet preserves Jiang's explanation of his own search and confusion; it should not be promoted as a factual evaluation of Holocaust documentation.
